planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the growing season.

directions: Root stem cuttings in a well-drained mix and grow in bright indirect light to keep the blue-green leaves marked with their characteristic purple-brown blotches. Water only when the soil dries out. Clusters of white flowers rise on tall stems in winter and spring.

planting.note: Plantlets from the leaf margins root wherever they fall.

directions: Simply press one of the tiny plantlets that line the leaf edges onto soil and it will root on its own. Grow in bright light and water only when dry. This plant self-sows aggressively from dropped plantlets and can become weedy, so contain it and note that all parts are toxic if eaten.

planting.note: Tip-borne plantlets root readily wherever they drop.

directions: Plantlets form at the tips of the narrow tubular leaves and root easily once set on soil. Grow in bright light with infrequent watering. Whorls of orange-red bell flowers appear in winter atop tall stems, but the plant spreads invasively from plantlets and is toxic, so keep it in a controlled container.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Root a callused cutting in fast-draining soil and give it strong light to keep the cream, green, and pink variegation vivid. Water only when the soil dries completely, and prune out any all-green shoots that revert. This slow variegated jade forms a small shrub and may bloom white to pink in winter when mature.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Set callused cuttings in a fast-draining mix and grow in full sun to deepen the red edges on the powdery, chalk-coated paddle leaves. Water sparingly and shelter from frost. A mature rosette produces a tall spike of fragrant tubular yellow flowers, then that rosette declines while offsets carry on.

planting.note: Divide clumps or set out transplants in early spring.

directions: Plant divisions in lean, well-drained soil and full sun to keep the upright stems from flopping. The blue-green foliage forms a tidy mound through summer. Broad flower heads open bright rose-pink in late summer and buzz with bees and butterflies; leave the seed heads for winter interest.

planting.note: Surface-sow seed or lay rooted stems in spring.

directions: Press stems or scatter fine seed on well-drained soil where the slender pointed leaves can knit into a low carpet. It spreads quickly and roots along the stems as it goes. Yellow star flowers cover the mat in early summer, and it works well as a filler between pavers.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Root a callused cutting in fast-draining soil and grow it in bright light for a compact, small-leaved jade with red-tinged margins. Water only when the soil dries out completely to protect the stems from rot. This dwarf jade stays smaller than the species and can bloom white to pink in winter with age.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or early summer once the cut end has callused.

directions: Let a cut rosette air-dry for a few days, then press the stem an inch into gritty, fast-draining mix and water sparingly until roots form. Give it strong light to keep the foliage nearly black rather than green. Rosettes deepen in color through the cool growing months and send up a yellow flower cone in spring.

planting.note: Take cuttings in spring; seed is slow and rarely used at home.

directions: Plant callused cuttings in coarse, well-drained soil and keep them barely moist while rooting. Water more in fall and winter when the plant is actively growing, and ease off during summer dormancy. Mature branched stems carry glossy green rosettes that bloom in bright yellow domes.

planting.note: Propagate from rosette cuttings in spring or summer.

directions: Callus a rosette cutting for several days, then set it in gritty mix and water lightly until it anchors. Bright but slightly filtered light keeps the cream, green, and pink variegation vivid without scorching. This clone is monocarpic, so the flowering rosette dies after blooming while side shoots carry on.

planting.note: Root branch cuttings in spring; this species offsets freely.

directions: Snip a well-branched stem, let it callus, and root it in sharp-draining soil with sparing water. Its small blue-green rosettes edged in red form a dense, shrubby mound that tolerates neglect. Loose sprays of pale creamy-yellow flowers appear on mature clumps in spring.

planting.note: Sow the fine seed on the surface of gritty mix in spring.

directions: Scatter seed on damp, gritty mix, do not cover it, and keep humidity high until sprouts appear. Grow the flat, plate-like rosette in bright filtered light and keep water off the crown to prevent rot. After several years it lifts a tall flower spike, blooms, and then dies.

planting.note: Divide clumping offsets in spring or early summer.

directions: Pull apart an offset with roots attached and set it into gritty, well-drained soil, watering lightly at first. This tough little aloe takes more cold than most and forms tidy clustering rosettes flecked with white. Slender orange flower spikes rise above the leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Separate offsets in spring; very forgiving in poor soil.

directions: Plant a rooted pup in lean, sharp-draining soil and water only when the ground is dry. Its broad leaves are boldly spotted and edged with soft teeth, forming spreading colonies over time. Flat-topped heads of orange to coral flowers appear over a long season and draw pollinators.

planting.note: Sow seed in spring in cool, gritty mix; this mountain species dislikes heat.

directions: Surface-sow seed on gritty mix and keep it lightly moist and cool while seedlings establish. Give it sharp drainage, some afternoon shade in hot regions, and more water than typical aloes since it hails from wet highlands. Mature plants form a striking five-rank spiral rosette and eventually raise dusky red flower spikes.

planting.note: Lift and divide crowded offsets in spring.

directions: Separate a pup and settle it into gritty soil, holding off on water for several days. Full sun brings out a blue-gray cast and coppery leaf edges as the small rosettes cluster into a mat. Short spikes of orange flowers rise in late spring.

planting.note: Divide the freely offsetting clumps in spring.

directions: Split off a rooted offset and plant it in gritty soil, watering lightly to establish. This tidy hybrid forms dense clumps of narrow blue-gray leaves and is prized for heavy, reliable bloom. Orange flower spikes appear over a long winter-to-spring window and attract hummingbirds.

planting.note: Separate offsets in spring; forms tight clusters.

directions: Remove a pup and plant it in sharp-draining mix, keeping it dry for the first several days. Its short blue-green leaves are lined with soft white teeth and form dense little rosettes. Slim orange flower spikes rise well above the compact clump in spring.

planting.note: Divide offsets or root a single leaf in spring or summer.

directions: Detach an offset or callus a whole leaf, then set it in gritty mix and water lightly once rooting begins. This popular hybrid has thick, tongue-shaped leaves marbled with pale warty bands and takes low light well. Arching stems of curved pink-and-green flowers appear on established plants.

planting.note: Separate clustering offsets in spring.

directions: Plant a rooted offset in fast-draining soil and keep it in bright, indirect light to avoid leaf scorch. The strap-shaped, glossy leaves are mottled with lighter green and form a low fan or rosette. Long wands of tubular pink-to-green flowers arch out in spring.

planting.note: Divide offsets or root leaves in spring and summer.

directions: Set an offset or callused leaf into gritty mix and water lightly until roots form. The thick, tapering leaves are densely covered in white raised warts and take bright, filtered light best. Slender arching stems bear small pink tubular flowers in spring.

planting.note: Separate offsets in spring; slow but steady.

directions: Pull a rooted offset from the clump and pot it in gritty mix, watering only after it settles. Bright indirect light keeps the crisp white zebra bands across the dark leaves bold and tight. Thin stalks of small tubular white flowers rise well above the compact rosette.

planting.note: Divide the readily offsetting clumps in spring.

directions: Detach an offset and plant it shallowly in fast-draining mix, keeping it barely moist. This forgiving houseplant tolerates lower light and is studded with raised white dots along dark green leaves. Established rosettes send up wiry stems of tiny white blooms.

planting.note: Separate offsets in spring; keep out of harsh sun.

directions: Plant a small offset in gritty mix and give it bright, filtered light so the leaf tips stay plump and translucent. The soft, rounded leaves have windowed tips that glow when backlit, and the clump stays low and dense. Fine stalks of little white flowers appear from the center in the growing season.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring or root single leaves.

directions: Set an offset into sharp-draining mix and water sparingly, letting it dry fully between drinks. The fleshy leaves flatten back at the tips into translucent triangular windows arranged in a star. Slim spikes of small white tubular flowers rise above the rosette.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings or divide offsets in spring.

directions: Plant an offset or callused leaf in gritty, mineral mix and water lightly on a dry-between cycle. The stubby leaves are cut off flat at the top with translucent windows, sitting in a curious two-ranked row. Give bright indirect light and expect thin spikes of pale flowers on mature plants.

planting.note: Divide clustering offsets in spring.

directions: Separate a rooted pup and pot it in fast-draining mix, watering only when the soil is dry. Its stiff, triangular leaves are ridged with raised ribs that give a washboard texture and form flat rosettes. Wiry stems carry small white flowers in the warm season.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; give it full sun and space.

directions: Plant a rooted pup in lean, fast-draining soil and hold back on water once established. The bold blue-gray leaves are edged in creamy yellow and armed with sharp marginal teeth, so site it away from paths. Like the species it is monocarpic, throwing a towering flower spike after many years before dying.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; protect from hard frost.

directions: Set a pup in well-drained soil and water lightly until established, giving a little afternoon shade in hot climates. This spineless agave forms a soft rosette of pale green leaves on a short trunk, safe near walkways. After years it arches out a long, curving flower spike that gives the plant its foxtail name.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; one of the more cold-tolerant agaves.

directions: Plant a pup in gritty, very well-drained soil and keep it dry in winter to help it shrug off cold. The compact blue-gray rosette of broad leaves tipped with dark spines resembles a tight artichoke. After many years a tall branched stalk of yellow flowers rises before the rosette dies.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in fast-draining soil.

directions: Settle a pup into gritty soil and water sparingly once it roots. Its narrow dark-green leaves carry fine white margins that peel into curling threads, giving the rosette a hairy look. A tall unbranched flower spike appears after several years before the rosette dies back.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; protect from frost.

directions: Set a pup into gritty, well-drained soil and water lightly until it establishes. The smooth, gently arching leaves are soft green striped with yellow margins, forming a graceful vase-shaped rosette. It flowers only after several years, raising a tall stalk before the mother plant declines.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; tolerant of cold and some moisture.

directions: Plant a pup in gritty but not bone-dry soil, as this high-elevation species accepts more moisture than most agaves. Its broad, deep-green leaves press together and leave crisp bud imprints as they unfurl into a large symmetrical rosette. A tall flower spike eventually rises after many years of growth.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in very sharp-draining soil.

directions: Plant a pup in gritty soil and water sparingly, giving it full sun to keep the form tight. Its many narrow, needle-tipped leaves radiate in all directions to make a spiky ball that clusters over time. A tall slender flower spike rises from mature rosettes.

planting.note: Sow seed in warm gritty mix in spring.

directions: Press seed into warm, well-drained mix and keep barely moist until seedlings appear. This miniature agave forms a small rosette of dark leaves lined with white markings and curling filaments. Even the flower spike is modest, rising a couple of feet with pale yellow blooms after several years.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Set a pup into fast-draining soil and water only when dry once it establishes. Its narrow green leaves often carry a paler central stripe and are edged with sharp teeth, forming an open rosette that offsets into colonies. A tall flower spike rises from mature plants before that rosette fades.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or summer.

directions: Callus a stem cutting for a few days, then root it in gritty, well-drained mix with light watering. This species builds a single tall stem topped by a large glossy green rosette rather than branching freely. After a few years it lifts a big yellow flower cone, and that flowering rosette then dies.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in lean, gritty soil.

directions: Plant a rooted pup in poor, fast-draining soil and water only occasionally once established. This robust form makes a large open rosette of gray-green, spine-margined leaves and offsets to form colonies. It flowers just once after many years, raising a tall branched stalk before the rosette dies.

planting.note: Set out or repot in warm spring weather once nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Sow seed thinly on a gritty mineral mix, press in without covering, and keep warm and humid until the tiny green globes appear. Grow lean in a fast-draining pot and water only when the soil dries completely, tapering off through winter. This globe stays solitary and takes many years to reach the size where a ring of small yellow flowers forms at the crown in summer.

planting.note: Pot up or set out in late spring when warmth returns.

directions: Surface-sow on a gritty mix and keep warm and lightly moist until germination, then grow on bright and dry. The white woolly hairs need protection from overhead water, so irrigate at the base only when the soil is fully dry. Give a cool, dry winter rest to trigger the crown ring of pink-purple flowers in spring.

planting.note: Detach and root offsets during warm weather for easy new plants.

directions: Twist off a finger-like stem, let the cut end callus for several days, then set it in barely moist gritty mix to root. Grow bright and dry, watering only when the soil dries out and holding back in winter. Small pale yellow to pink flowers ring the stem tips in spring after a cool dormancy.

planting.note: Root detached pads in warm weather; handle carefully because of the fine barbed glochids.

directions: Snap off a mature pad, let it callus for a week, then stand it upright in dry gritty mix and water sparingly once roots form. Wear gloves, since the tufts of tiny golden glochids detach and irritate skin at the slightest touch. Grow it bright and dry; established plants may open pale yellow flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant out in warm weather in a sharply drained, sunny spot.

directions: Sow on warm gritty mix and keep bright, moving seedlings on slowly as they build their heavy hooked spines. Grow in full sun with excellent drainage and let the soil dry completely between waterings. Mature barrels lean toward the sun and crown themselves with orange-to-yellow flowers followed by yellow fruit in late summer.

planting.note: Loose offsets root almost anywhere they land in warm weather.

directions: Set a detached thimble-shaped offset on dry gritty mix, where it roots readily with almost no attention. Grow bright and dry and water only when the soil is fully dry, keeping it nearly bone dry in winter. Small creamy-white flowers appear near the stem tips in spring on well-rested plants.

planting.note: Repot in warm weather; the colorful grafted forms lack chlorophyll and rely on a rootstock.

directions: Surface-sow on a gritty mix and keep warm and humid until the small ribbed globes appear, then grow on in bright but not scorching light. Water when the soil dries and give a dry winter rest. Green-bodied plants open pale pink or greenish flowers in spring, while the bright red and yellow moon-cactus forms are grafted onto a green rootstock.

planting.note: Root large pads in spring for the fastest path to a fruiting plant.

directions: Callus a whole pad for a week, then set the base an inch or two into gritty soil and water lightly until rooted. Grow in full sun with sparing water; established plants become large and shrubby. Yellow-to-orange flowers open in spring, ripening into the sweet reddish or greenish fruit known as tuna.

planting.note: Root the flat blue-gray pads in warm, dry weather.

directions: Stand a callused pad in fast-draining mineral soil and water only lightly until roots establish. This spineless-looking species still carries glochids, so handle it with care. Grow hot and dry for the vivid magenta-pink flowers that open in spring.

planting.note: Pot up in spring; blooms young and freely.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix, keep warm until germination, then grow on bright and lean. Water when dry and rest cool and dry over winter. This small clustering cactus is prized for the near-complete ring of bright pink-purple flowers it opens around the crown in spring.

planting.note: Grow warm and bright; the blue skin colors best in strong sun.

directions: Sow on warm gritty mix and grow seedlings on slowly in bright light. Water only when the soil dries and keep it dry through winter to protect the powdery blue skin. Mature barrels ring the crown with small clear-yellow flowers in summer.

planting.note: Repot in warm weather in a sharply drained mineral mix.

directions: Sow warm and grow the flattened globe on lean and bright, where it develops broad hooked central spines. Water sparingly and only when dry, resting it in winter. Purple to pink funnel-shaped flowers can open at the crown in autumn on mature plants.

planting.note: Water only at the base to keep the feathery down clean and dry.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and keep warm until the soft-spined seedlings appear. The feathery white covering hates overhead water, so irrigate at the soil only when fully dry and keep the plant dry in winter. Small cream-colored flowers nestle among the down in late autumn to winter.

planting.note: Tolerates a little cold if kept dry; repot in spring.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the small dark green globe in bright but not scorching light. Water when the soil dries and rest cool and dry over winter. It is unusual for its genus in opening deep red flowers in summer.

planting.note: One of the cold-hardiest cacti; the small pads detach and root readily.

directions: Press or lay a small detached pad on gritty, fast-draining soil and it usually roots on its own. Keep the site dry in winter, when the low pads shrivel to survive extreme cold. Pale yellow flowers open in early summer on well-established mats.

planting.note: Very cold-hardy; root pads in spring in a dry, sunny bed.

directions: Callus a pad and set it on sharply drained soil, watering only sparingly until it anchors. Keep it dry through winter, when the densely spined pads flatten against the ground. Yellow to pink flowers open in early summer above the spiny mat.

planting.note: Root large pads in spring for a fast-growing shrubby plant.

directions: Callus a pad for a week, then set its base into gritty soil and water sparingly until rooted. It grows into a large clumping shrub in full sun with little care. Yellow to orange flowers open in spring, followed by the deep red-purple fruit.

planting.note: Grow hot and dry; cold and drought stress deepen the purple pad color.

directions: Root a callused pad in sharply drained soil, watering lightly until it takes hold. Full sun with winter chill turns the rounded blue-gray pads a striking lavender-purple. Bright yellow flowers open in spring on established plants.

planting.note: Repot in warm weather; forms handsome clusters with age.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and keep warm until germination, then grow bright and lean. The white-wooled body carries hooked spines, so water at the base only when dry. Pink to purplish flowers ring the crown in spring after a cool winter rest.

planting.note: Grow bright; spine color varies from white to reddish-brown.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the densely spined cylinder on in strong light. Water when dry and keep nearly dry through a cool winter. Deep pink to purple-red flowers form a ring near the crown in spring.

planting.note: Very slow and long-lived; grow warm and dry.

directions: Sow on warm gritty mix and grow the ribbed seedling on slowly in bright light. Water only when the soil dries fully and keep it dry over winter. This massive, extremely long-lived barrel eventually ropes small yellow flowers around a woolly crown.

planting.note: Desert species that wants full sun and very sharp drainage.

directions: Sow warm and grow seedlings on lean and bright as they build their reddish curved spines. Give full sun with excellent drainage and let the soil dry completely between waterings. Yellow to orange flowers ring the crown in late spring to summer.

planting.note: Grow hot and bright for the strongest red spines.

directions: Sow on warm gritty mix and grow the young columns on in full sun to intensify the bright red spines. Water only when dry and keep the plant dry over winter. Small orange-yellow flowers cluster at the crown in summer on mature plants.

planting.note: Desert barrel for full sun and dry, gritty soil.

directions: Sow warm and grow the ribbed globe on slowly in strong light with stout hooked central spines. Water only when the soil dries and rest it dry through winter. Yellow to red flowers open around the crown in summer.

planting.note: Slow and drought-loving; needs very sharp mineral drainage.

directions: Sow on warm gritty mix and grow the blue-gray globe on very slowly in bright light. Water sparingly and only when bone dry, giving it a strict dry winter. Pink flowers open at the crown in summer on this compact, long-lived barrel.

planting.note: Grow in bright, indirect light rather than harsh full sun.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the smooth green globe in bright but filtered light. Water when the soil dries and keep it dry over winter. It is named for the spider-like spines pressed against the body and opens large white flowers in spring and summer.

planting.note: One of the largest chin cacti; grow bright and dry.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the broad ribbed globe on in bright light. Water when the soil dries and rest it dry in winter. This large chin cactus develops stout curved spines and opens small white to pale pink flowers near the crown in summer.

planting.note: Small clustering species that tolerates cool, dry winters.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the tiny clustering globes in bright, gentle light. Water when dry and keep dry over a cool winter, which this hardy species tolerates well. Pale pink flowers open early in spring, often before other cacti wake up.

planting.note: Forms fluffy white clusters; water at the base to keep the wool dry.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the silky white-haired clusters on bright and lean. Hooked spines hide under the wool, so water at the base only when dry. Small cream to pink flowers ring the stems in spring after a cool winter rest.

planting.note: A single-spined form grown for its reddish spine crown.

directions: Surface-sow or grow on nursery plants in bright light, watering only when the soil dries. This single-central-spine selection shows a warm reddish crown of spines. Deep pink flowers ring the top in spring after a dry, cool winter rest.

planting.note: Spine color ranges from yellow to reddish; grow bright.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the cylindrical stems on in strong light. Water when the soil dries and rest cool and dry over winter. Bright magenta-pink flowers form a ring near the crown in spring and summer.

planting.note: Forms broad clumps of many heads with age.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow bright and lean, letting the plant branch into a wide clump. Water when the soil dries and keep it dry through winter. Small purplish-pink flowers ring the crowns in spring.

planting.note: Easy chin cactus for bright, filtered light.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the ribbed green globe on in bright, indirect light. Water when the soil dries and keep dry in winter. White to pale pink flowers open at the crown through spring and summer.

planting.note: Long stiff spines and showy flowers; grow bright.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the spiny globe on in bright light. Water when the soil dries and rest it dry over winter. Large pinkish-lilac flowers open at the crown in late spring and summer.

planting.note: A pale-spined form of the golden barrel; same slow, dry culture.

directions: Sow on gritty mineral mix, keep warm and humid until germination, then grow lean and bright. Water only when the soil dries completely and keep it dry through winter. This whitish-spined form is as slow as the type, ringing its woolly crown with small yellow flowers only after many years.

planting.note: Offsets freely into dense mats; easy from detached heads.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix or root detached offsets on dry soil, where they establish quickly. Grow bright and lean, watering only when dry and holding back in winter. Small creamy-yellow flowers are followed by long-lasting red berries around the clustered heads.

planting.note: Slender stems lean and sprawl as they lengthen.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix and grow the slim cylindrical stems on in bright light. Water when the soil dries and rest cool and dry over winter. Bright pink flowers form neat rings around the stems in spring.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost danger passes into sharply drained ground.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep it lightly moist and warm until sprouts appear, then move to bright light. Grow in gritty, fast-draining soil and water only when the top inch is dry, since standing moisture rots the succulent stems. Expect a long carpet of magenta daisy-like flowers from early summer into fall in full sun.

planting.note: Transplant into lean, gravelly soil once the ground has warmed.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of gritty mix and keep barely damp until germination. This is one of the toughest mat-forming ice plants, so plant it where drainage is perfect and let the fleshy foliage take on red winter tints. Bright golden-yellow flowers blanket the mat in late spring on the first warm days.

planting.note: Set out plants once the soil warms in spring.

directions: Plant rooted pieces in lean, gritty soil with excellent drainage and full sun exposure. Water lightly during establishment, then let the mat fend for itself and keep it dry over winter. Soft salmon-pink flowers bloom over a long season from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors into gravelly ground after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on gritty mix and keep warm and lightly moist to sprout. Grow in fast-draining soil in full sun and water only when dry, avoiding soggy winter conditions. Warm salmon-red to coral flowers appear in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant seedlings out into sharply drained soil after frost.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep it warm and moist until germination, then grow on in bright light. Set out into gritty soil in full sun and water modestly, tapering off before winter. Pink flowers with distinct white eyes bloom heavily the first summer from an early sowing.

planting.note: Plant small divisions into very gritty soil in spring.

directions: Tuck the tiny blue-gray finger-leaved tufts into a scree or trough with razor-sharp drainage. Keep it lean and dry, as this alpine mesemb hates rich soil and winter moisture. Bright magenta-pink flowers cover the little cushion very early in spring.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings into gravelly soil after the last frost.

directions: Plant rooted pieces in gritty, fast-draining soil and water lightly while they knit in. Give full sun and keep the crown dry through winter to prevent rot. Deep reddish-purple flowers open over a long summer season above the green mat.

common_name: Jewel of Desert Garnet Ice Plant

planting.note: Plant nursery starts into sharply drained soil after frost.

directions: Set the compact plants into gritty soil in full sun and firm them gently. Water sparingly and ensure winter drainage is excellent to keep the mat alive. Deep red flowers with pale centers cover the low cushion from spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant out only once nights stay warm and frost is past.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix, keep warm, and grow seedlings on in bright light before hardening off. Plant in fast-draining soil in full sun and water sparingly once established, as it thrives on neglect. Vivid orange flowers blaze open on sunny days in late spring, closing at night and in cloud.

planting.note: Transplant to a warm, sunny slope after frost danger ends.

directions: Start seed warm on gritty mix or take cuttings, which root readily. Plant on a sunny bank in sharply drained soil where it can spread and cascade. Brilliant magenta-pink flowers cover the plant in a dazzling spring sheet during full sun.

planting.note: Set plants out into warm, well-drained soil after frost.

directions: Germinate seed warm on the surface of gritty mix, or root soft cuttings in spring. Grow in full sun and lean, fast-draining soil, watering only during dry spells. Soft pale-pink flowers smother the mounded shrublet in spring.

planting.note: Plant into sharply drained soil once frost has passed.

directions: Sow warm on gritty mix or root cuttings, keeping media barely moist. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and avoid overwatering, which invites rot. The blue-gray foliage disappears under a spring blanket of lilac to purple flowers.

planting.note: Usually grown as a potted houseplant kept above freezing.

directions: Sprinkle the fine seed on gritty mix, keep warm and humid until sprouted, then ease off moisture. Grow in a shallow pot of very fast-draining soil, water only when fully dry, and keep it dry in winter. The toothed leaf pairs resemble open jaws, and large glossy yellow flowers open on sunny autumn afternoons.

planting.note: Grow as a tender container succulent, protected from frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty compost, cover lightly, and keep warm until seedlings establish. Provide bright light and sparse watering, letting the mix dry completely between drinks. The soft-toothed jawed leaves form clumps and produce bright yellow flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Keep as a frost-free potted plant year round.

directions: Sow fine seed on the surface of gritty mix and keep gently moist and warm to germinate. Grow in a shallow container with sharp drainage and water only after the soil is bone dry. The plump toothed leaf pairs clump readily and open golden-yellow flowers in fall.

planting.note: Best grown in a pot brought indoors before frost.

directions: Scatter seed on gritty compost, keep warm and humid to sprout, then reduce moisture sharply. The dark green leaves are studded with white warty tubercles and edged with soft teeth. Grow dry and bright, and enjoy large yellow flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Grow as a windowsill succulent kept above freezing.

directions: Sow the dust-fine seed on gritty mix, mist and cover to hold humidity, and keep warm and bright until sprouts appear. Water very sparingly and never during the winter or leaf-renewal period, when the plant lives on the old leaves. These brown-topped living stones take two to three years to flower, opening yellow blooms in autumn.

planting.note: Keep as a frost-free potted plant on a bright sill.

directions: Surface-sow onto sharp mineral mix and keep gently humid and warm to germinate the tiny seed. Grow in strong light and water only in active spring and fall growth, keeping it completely dry while the new body forms. The tan, dimpled tops split to reveal golden flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Grow indoors in bright light, safe from any frost.

directions: Sow fine seed on gritty mineral compost, cover to keep humid, and give bright warmth until seedlings appear. Water minimally and withhold entirely as the old leaves shrivel and feed the new pair. Grayish stone-like bodies produce white flowers in fall after a couple of years.

planting.note: A prized red form kept as a frost-free houseplant.

directions: Start the fine seed warm and humid on mineral grit and give the brightest light you can once sprouted. Keep watering very light and cease it during leaf renewal to avoid rot. The bodies are a striking deep red-purple, and white flowers open in autumn on mature plants.

planting.note: Container succulent that must be kept above freezing.

directions: Sow onto sharp gritty mix, keep humid and warm to sprout, then grow hard in strong light. Water only in spring and autumn and stay dry over winter while the leaves are replaced. The rusty-patterned tops split open to reveal white flowers in fall.

planting.note: One of the more forgiving Lithops, still kept frost-free.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed on mineral grit, keep humid until germination, then reduce moisture. This species tolerates a little more water than most but still needs a dry winter rest. Gray-green bodies with muted windows produce white flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Grow bright and frost-free in a gritty pot.

directions: Sow fine seed on mineral compost and keep warm and humid until the seedlings root in. Give maximum light and water only lightly during active growth, staying dry in winter. The pale gray marbled tops open pure white flowers in fall.

planting.note: Bright windowsill succulent, protect from frost.

directions: Start seed warm and humid on gritty mineral mix, then grow in the strongest light available. Unlike most Lithops this one flowers in summer, so keep it especially dry through the hottest months. Withhold all water during winter leaf renewal, and expect yellow flowers on mature plants.

planting.note: Keep as a frost-free potted stone plant.

directions: Sow the fine seed on sharp grit and keep humid and warm until germination, then grow hard. Water sparingly in spring and fall and keep bone dry through winter renewal. The rusty-brown wrinkled tops produce golden-yellow flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Grow bright and dry, always above freezing.

directions: Surface-sow onto mineral grit, keep humid to germinate, and then give the brightest position. Water only during active growth and never while the new leaf pair emerges. Gray bodies with darker mouth lines open white flowers in autumn.

planting.note: A frost-free container stone plant for a sunny sill.

directions: Sow fine seed on gritty compost and keep humid and warm until sprouts appear. Provide intense light and minimal water, ceasing entirely during winter leaf renewal. The reddish-brown patterned tops open yellow flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Keep bright and frost-free year round.

directions: Start seed warm and humid on mineral grit and grow the seedlings in strong light. Water very lightly during spring and fall growth and keep it dry over winter. The tan bodies flecked with darker dots open yellow flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Grow as a bright, frost-free potted succulent.

directions: Sow the dust-fine seed on gritty mix, hold humidity until germination, and then grow hard in full light. Water sparingly in the growing seasons and withhold it during leaf change. The pale bodies with reddish windows open bright yellow flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Frost-free windowsill succulent.

directions: Sow fine seed on sharp mineral grit and keep it humid and warm until sprouting. The tops are dotted with tiny red warts, unusual among living stones. Water only in active growth, stay dry in winter, and enjoy flowers that may be yellow or reddish in autumn.

planting.note: Grow as a frost-free pot succulent in bright light.

directions: Sow seed on gritty mineral mix, keep humid and warm until it germinates, then reduce moisture. The plant looks like a cracked gray-brown stone and rots easily if overwatered, so water only when the soil is fully dry. Large daisy-like flowers, usually orange, open in late winter to spring.

planting.note: Bright, frost-free container plant.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on gritty mix and keep humid and warm until seedlings appear. The club-shaped leaves stand upright with translucent windowed tips and clump into little colonies. Grow bright and dry, water sparingly, and look for white to pale-yellow flowers in fall.

planting.note: Grow in a gritty pot kept above freezing.

directions: Sow fine seed on sharp mineral mix, keep humid to germinate, then grow in strong light. The blue-gray leaves are tipped with rough warty bumps that mimic limestone gravel. Keep it dry and bright, watering lightly in cool seasons, and expect yellow to orange flowers in winter.

planting.note: Plant out or root cuttings once frost has passed.

directions: Root the easy cuttings in gritty mix or sow seed warm on the surface. This fast trailing mat has bright green heart-shaped leaves and spreads quickly in warm sun. Water occasionally in dry spells and enjoy small magenta-pink flowers on and off through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant rooted stems on a sunny bank after frost; can be invasive.

directions: Lay cuttings on gritty soil where they root quickly, or sow seed warm. This vigorous mat has large three-sided leaves and spreads fast, so give it room and site it away from wild areas where it can escape. Big yellow-to-pink flowers open on sunny days from spring into summer, followed by edible fig-like fruits.

planting.note: Plant on a sunny slope for erosion control after frost.

directions: Root cuttings easily in gritty mix or start seed warm on the surface. The tiny glistening-leaved stems form a dense cascading mat ideal for banks and walls. Water sparingly once established, and expect a spectacular spring sheet of pale-pink flowers.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings into sharp soil after frost.

directions: Root the easy stem cuttings in gritty mix in spring. The chalky blue-gray toothed leaves grow on reddish stems that trail attractively over pots and walls. Grow in full sun with sparse water, and enjoy fragrant pink flowers in spring.

planting.note: Grow as a bright, frost-free pot plant.

directions: Sow fine seed on gritty mineral mix, keep humid and warm to germinate, then grow bright. The upright club-shaped leaves have flat windowed tops much like baby toes but are unrelated. Water carefully and keep dry in winter, and look for magenta-pink flowers with white centers in summer.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring once the cut end has callused for several days.

directions: Let a fresh cutting dry and callus for three to seven days, then insert it an inch or two deep into gritty, fast-draining mix and support it upright. Water sparingly until roots form and only when the soil is fully dry thereafter. New triangular stems branch upward as the plant establishes; wear gloves because the milky sap is a skin and eye irritant.

planting.note: Take pencil-thin stem cuttings in warm weather and let them callus before planting.

directions: Callus cuttings for a week, then set them in dry, sharp-draining soil and withhold water until roots appear. Bright light and cool nights push the coral and gold coloring on the young stems. The copious white sap is toxic and caustic, so handle only with gloves and eye protection.

planting.note: Root branch cuttings in spring after the cut surface has dried.

directions: Allow the ghostly white cutting to callus, then plant it shallowly in a mineral-rich, quick-drying mix. Give bright but slightly filtered light to protect the near-chlorophyll-free stems from scorching, and water only when the pot is bone dry. The candelabra branches thicken slowly; keep it warm and dry through winter.

planting.note: Surface-sow fresh seed in warm, gritty mix in spring.

directions: Press fresh seed onto a moist, sandy surface and keep it warm and lightly humid until it sprouts, then ease off water as seedlings firm up. This plant is a solitary segmented globe and cannot be cut for propagation, so seed is the only route. Plants are male or female, and both are needed for viable seed; grow lean and dry to preserve the tidy shape.

planting.note: Detach and root offset stems in spring after callusing.

directions: Twist off a knobby side stem, let it dry for several days, and set it in dry, coarse mix until it roots. Bright sun and cool winter temperatures bring out a purplish blush on the ribbed stems. It clumps freely into a low colony; keep it dry in the cold months to prevent rot.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in warm, sandy mix, or root offsets from clumping plants.

directions: Barely cover seed in a gritty medium and keep warm until germination, then grow hard in full sun. This ribbed gray-green barrel resembles a cactus but exudes toxic latex when cut. Older plants offset at the base and can be divided; water lightly in growth and keep dry and cool over winter.

planting.note: Root segment cuttings in summer after a long callusing period.

directions: Cut at a joint, rinse away the sap, and callus the piece a full week before planting in fast-draining soil. Long horn-like spines guard the wavy triangular stems, so handle with heavy gloves. Water moderately in warm growth and keep nearly dry through winter to prevent basal rot.

planting.note: Sow the self-ejected seed it drops, or start purchased seed warm in spring.

directions: Sow fresh seed shallowly in warm, gritty mix; this plant famously flings its own seed and seedlings often appear in nearby pots. It tolerates a bit more water and light shade than most succulent spurges. Prized for the white-veined leaves atop a spiny trunk, it stays evergreen indoors with steady warmth.

planting.note: Detach and root a snaking arm, or divide clumps in spring.

directions: Pull off one of the trailing green arms, callus it briefly, and lay it on gritty soil until it roots at the base. A central caudex radiates dozens of finger-like stems into a living Medusa head. Small yellow-green cyathia rim the arm tips in the growing season; keep it lean, bright, and dry in winter.

planting.note: Root basal offsets in spring after callusing the cut.

directions: Remove a side branch, let the sap dry over several days, and set it in dry, mineral soil until rooted. Bright red spine-like flower stalks stud the ribbed green stems and give the plant its pincushion look. It clumps into a low thicket; water sparingly in growth and keep dry when cold.

planting.note: Root large stem cuttings in the warm season after callusing.

directions: Callus a thick cutting for a week or more, then plant it upright in coarse, dry soil and stake it while it roots. The spiraled ribs carry small paddle leaves near the growing tips during warm weather. It makes a fast branching hedge in frost-free climates; the latex is a strong irritant.

planting.note: Divide clumps or root cut stems in spring after callusing.

directions: Separate a rooted section from the cushion or callus a cut stem, then plant into sharp-draining soil kept dry until established. This Moroccan spurge forms dense four-angled mounds and is hardier to brief cold than most. Its dried latex is the historic source of the compound resiniferatoxin, so keep the caustic sap off skin.

planting.note: Root branch cuttings in the warm season after a long callus.

directions: Callus a cut branch well, then plant it upright in gritty, dry soil and support it until anchored. Creamy marbling washes over the winged green stems, brightest in strong light. It grows into a tall candelabra tree where frost-free; protect the variegated tissue from harsh midday scorch and keep dry in winter.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed warm; both sexes are needed for seed to set.

directions: Barely cover fresh seed in sandy mix and keep it warm and lightly moist until sprouting, then grow lean and bright. This ribbed green globe resembles a small melon and is usually solitary, so seed is the main increase. Persistent dried flower stalks fringe the crown; keep it dry and cool through the resting months.

planting.note: Root basal offsets in spring after callusing the cut face.

directions: Separate a rooted pup or callus a cut offset, then plant into dry, gritty soil and water only after roots form. Many closely set ribs give the whitish-frosted stem its snowflake look, deepest in strong sun. Dark flower stalks edge the crown in the growing season; keep it lean and nearly dry in winter.

planting.note: Root branch cuttings in warm weather after callusing.

directions: Cut a segment at a joint, rinse the sap, and callus it before setting into dry, coarse mix. The winged stems are marbled yellow-green and grow in a distinctive zig-zag with short paired spines. It branches into an upright clump; give bright light, water moderately in growth, and keep dry in winter.

planting.note: Root large branch cuttings in the warm season after callusing.

directions: Callus a heavy cut branch for a week or more, then plant it upright in dry, sharp soil and brace it until rooted. In frost-free ground it becomes a massive many-armed tree with dark green four-angled branches. The abundant latex is highly toxic and caustic; site it away from paths and handle only with full protection.

planting.note: Root branch cuttings in the warm season after callusing.

directions: Callus a cut branch, then plant it in dry, gritty soil and water only lightly until it roots. The plant forms a dense, intricately branched crown of spine-tipped silvery-gray twigs. It makes a striking specimen or living fence in warm climates; keep it in strong sun and dry through the cool season.

planting.note: Root branch cuttings in warm weather after a long callus.

directions: Callus a cut branch thoroughly, then set it into dry, coarse soil and stake it while roots develop. Segmented, heart-shaped ribbed branches stack up a central trunk into a candelabra form. It is fast and dramatic where frost never comes; the latex is dangerously caustic, so wear eye protection when cutting.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed warm; this species is grown almost entirely from seed.

directions: Sow fresh seed shallowly in gritty mix and keep it warm and lightly moist until it sprouts. The knobby cone-like stem is crowned with a rosette of narrow deciduous leaves and looks strikingly like a small pineapple. It rests in winter by dropping its leaves; keep it dry and cool then and resume light watering as new leaves emerge.

planting.note: Divide the spreading rooted stems in spring.

directions: Lift and separate a rooted length of the creeping stem, then replant into gritty, fast-draining mix. This low Madagascan spurge spreads by underground rhizomes and carries wavy-edged crinkled leaves. Give bright but slightly filtered light and keep it barely moist in growth, nearly dry when cool.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed warm, or divide clustered stems.

directions: Sow fresh seed shallowly in a warm, gritty medium and keep lightly humid until germination. Collectors prize the wildly varied leaf colors and patterns, from bronze to pink-mottled, held on a short caudex-like base. Grow it warm in bright filtered light and water sparingly, keeping it dry through the cool rest period.

planting.note: Lay stem cuttings on the soil surface in spring to root at the nodes.

directions: Coil a cutting on top of gritty mix and press the beads gently into contact so they root at each node. Give bright, indirect light and let the soil dry fully between light waterings, since the pea-like beads store water. Trailing strands cascade over a pot rim; small brush-like white flowers with a cinnamon scent appear in the cool months.

planting.note: Root trailing stem cuttings on the soil surface in spring.

directions: Lay cuttings across gritty mix so the banana-shaped leaves and nodes touch the soil, then water lightly until rooted. It grows faster and is more forgiving than string of pearls, tolerating brighter light and occasional dryness. Vigorous strands trail long over a hanging pot; small white flowers appear in cooler weather.

planting.note: Surface-root stem cuttings on gritty mix in spring.

directions: Coil a cutting over the soil surface and let the tear-shaped beads root at their nodes before watering more freely. The plump gray-green beads often show fine translucent striping and darken in bright light. Give it indirect light and dry-down cycles; trailing strands drape well from a raised pot.

planting.note: Root the segmented stem pieces in spring after callusing.

directions: Let a jointed stem segment callus a couple of days, then set it upright in gritty mix and water sparingly until rooted. The blue-gray sausage-like segments give it the nickname hot dog cactus and carry deciduous lobed leaves in growth. It rests in summer heat; water it during the cooler growing months and keep dry when dormant.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or fall after callusing.

directions: Callus a cutting a few days, then plant it in gritty soil and water lightly until roots take hold. The upright blue-gray finger leaves carry a waxy bloom that intensifies the powdery color in full sun. It forms a spreading mound; keep it lean and dry, and small off-white flowers may appear in the cool season.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings directly into the ground or a pot after callusing.

directions: Callus cuttings briefly, then push them into gritty soil where they root readily and knit into a groundcover. The upright, slightly curved blue-gray fingers make one of the most vivid blue succulent mats for a dry garden. Give full sun and sharp drainage; it spreads steadily and shrugs off heat and drought once established.

planting.note: Root trailing stem cuttings on the soil surface in spring.

directions: Lay a cutting across gritty mix so its nodes touch the soil, then water lightly until it roots. The overlapping oval leaves flush purple-red in bright light and on cool, dry stress. Trailing stems spill over a hanging pot; orange-red brush flowers appear in the cool season. Also long sold as Senecio jacobsenii.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings directly into place after callusing.

directions: Callus cuttings briefly, then press them into gritty soil where they root and creep into a low mat. The short, upward-curving blue-gray fingers carry a chalky wax that glows in full sun. It is a compact groundcover for dry gardens; keep it lean and sharply drained and it needs little water once set.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in the warm season after callusing.

directions: Callus a cutting a few days, then set it in dry, gritty soil and water sparingly until rooted. The pale gray-green cylindrical stems carry small narrow leaves that drop in dry rest periods. It branches into an open shrub for frost-free gardens; give full sun and keep it dry when leafless.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring after the cut end calluses.

directions: Callus a cutting for several days, then plant it upright in fast-draining mix and support it while it roots. Strong light turns the leaves and stem edges a deep burgundy on this red form of the African milk tree. Water only when fully dry and keep it warm; the milky sap is a skin and eye irritant, so wear gloves.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed warm, or root detached arms after callusing.

directions: Barely cover fresh seed in gritty mix and keep it warm until it sprouts, then grow it lean and bright. A short central body sends out many radiating snake-like arms in a classic medusoid rosette. Keep it in strong sun with sharp drainage, water lightly in growth, and hold it dry through the cool rest.

planting.note: Start seed indoors six to eight weeks early and set plants out once nights stay above 55 F.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of a warm, moist mix without covering it, since light aids sprouting. Keep the plant pinched at the tips for a bushy habit and remove flower spikes to hold the leaf color. The foliage reaches its fullest patterning by midsummer.

planting.note: Transplant compact seedlings after the last spring frost into warm soil.

directions: Sow seed on the surface under bright light and hold at room temperature until the true leaves show their color. This dwarf strain branches well with little pinching, making it tidy for edging and containers. Expect a solid mound of mixed-color leaves within about ten weeks.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors only after frost, in a spot sheltered from harsh midday sun.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm and humid until germination, then grow on in strong light. The oversized red-and-green leaves scorch in full sun, so give the plant afternoon shade. It fills a large pot with bold foliage by late summer.

planting.note: Set out after frost in dappled shade to protect the broad leaves.

directions: Leave the seed uncovered on a warm mix and keep it evenly moist during sprouting. This selection carries huge rose-centered leaves edged in green and prefers a shaded, wind-free spot. Pinch early growth once to encourage a fuller plant.

planting.note: Transplant after all frost danger into a partly shaded bed.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and provide steady warmth and bright, indirect light. The large scarlet leaves color best out of direct sun, so choose a filtered-light location. Keep the mix moist but never soggy through the season.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; the bright leaves hold color in light shade.

directions: Sow on the surface, keep warm, and grow the seedlings in bright light. This slightly smaller Kong shows lime-green leaves splashed with maroon centers and lights up shady containers. Remove any bloom spikes to keep the foliage lush.

planting.note: Set out after frost in part shade for the deepest leaf color.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it warm and bright until seedlings appear. The deeply ruffled, near-black leaves carry a beet-red heart and stay richest with some shade. Pinch tips a couple of times for a dense, textured mound.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Scatter the seed on the surface of a warm mix and press it in lightly without covering. This old garden mix yields a wide range of leaf colors and shapes on medium plants. Pinch out flower stalks as they form to prolong the foliage display.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; this selection tolerates full sun.

directions: Root soft tip cuttings in moist media, then transplant once the danger of frost has passed. This vegetative sun coleus holds its rusty-orange color even in strong light and rarely flowers. Water regularly and pinch once if you want a bushier form.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in full to part sun.

directions: Take stem cuttings and root them in a warm, humid spot before moving them outdoors. The deep purple-black leaves make a strong backdrop and stay reliably in the sun. Keep the soil evenly moist and feed lightly for continued vigor.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings after frost into sun or light shade.

directions: Root tip cuttings in moist media and pot up before planting outdoors in warm weather. The chartreuse leaves glow in part shade and stay bright in morning sun. Pinch the growing tips for a rounder, fuller plant.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in full sun for the boldest red.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings rooted in warm, moist media before setting out. The solid deep-red foliage keeps its saturation in full sun and forms an upright mound. Water consistently and remove any flower spikes to keep it looking fresh.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in sun or part shade.

directions: Root cuttings in a humid environment and pot them before moving outdoors in warm soil. The scalloped copper-and-chartreuse leaves flush with burgundy on the undersides. Pinch back occasionally to maintain a dense, layered shape.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in sun to part shade.

directions: Take tip cuttings and root them before transplanting into warm garden soil. The leaves shift from rusty orange to gold with a rosy cast that deepens in more light. Keep evenly watered and pinch for fullness.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into part shade for richest color.

directions: Root soft cuttings in moist media, then plant out once nights are warm. The chocolate-brown leaves carry a bright green margin and glow in filtered light. Pinch the tips to build a broad, well-branched plant.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in sun or part shade.

directions: Propagate from tip cuttings in a warm, humid spot and transplant when frost has passed. The blended pink, rose, and green leaves recall ripe melon flesh and read best in bright shade. Water steadily and pinch to keep it compact.

planting.note: Transplant after frost; morning sun brightens the yellow leaves.

directions: Root cuttings in moist media before planting into warm soil. The golden-yellow leaves are edged and veined in maroon, giving a scalloped two-tone look. Give it bright light for the strongest color and pinch for density.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in sun to light shade.

directions: Take stem cuttings and root them in a warm place before transplanting outdoors. Each yellow leaf is randomly splashed with orange and rose, so no two are quite alike. Keep it moist and pinch the shoots to encourage branching.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in sun or part shade.

directions: Root tip cuttings in moist media and grow on before setting outdoors in warm weather. The bright green leaves are traced with bold, near-black veins for a netted effect. Pinch early growth and remove flower stems to keep the leaves prominent.

planting.note: Transplant compact seedlings after frost into rich soil.

directions: Surface-sow the seed under bright light and keep it warm until the seedlings color up. This dwarf Wizard shows solid velvety red leaves and stays tidy with little pinching. It forms a neat mound suited to borders and mixed pots.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in full to part sun.

directions: Root stem cuttings in a gritty mix and transplant once the soil has warmed. The large silvery, felted leaves add cool contrast and shrug off heat and dry spells. Water only when the top inch dries and pinch lightly to shape.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in bright, part shade.

directions: Root cuttings in moist media and grow on before planting in a partly shaded bed. The dark-backed green leaves are handsome all season, and shortening days trigger spikes of lavender bloom. Watch for the first flower stalks in early autumn.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in part shade; ideal for baskets.

directions: Root cuttings easily in water or moist mix and pot up before setting out. The scalloped green leaves are edged in creamy white and trail attractively over container rims. Pinch the tips to keep the trailing stems full rather than leggy.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in bright light for strong veining.

directions: Take tip cuttings and root them in a warm spot before planting outdoors. The green leaves are netted with golden-yellow veins on red stems for a bright contrast. Keep it moist and pinch often to encourage dense growth.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in sun to part shade.

directions: Root cuttings in moist media and grow them on before transplanting in warm soil. The leaves are an intense crimson with bright rose midribs that intensify in good light. Pinch the shoot tips to build a full, rounded plant.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in sun or light shade.

directions: Root cuttings in a humid environment and pot them before moving outdoors. This species carries narrow, pointed deep-red leaves for a slightly finer texture than common bloodleaf. Keep evenly moist and pinch to maintain a compact habit.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in sun to part shade.

directions: Propagate from tip cuttings rooted in moist media before setting out in warm soil. The rounded leaves show rich rose and burgundy tones and hold up well in heat and humidity. Pinch the tips to keep the plant dense and well shaped.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in full sun for deepest purple.

directions: Root stem cuttings in moist media, then transplant when the soil has warmed. The pointed, glossy leaves are a rich burgundy-purple that darkens in full sun. Its spreading, mounding habit fills borders and container edges quickly.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in full sun to part shade.

directions: Root cuttings in a warm, humid spot before planting into warm garden soil. This compact selection carries small deep-ruby leaves and stays low and dense. It works as a tidy edger or ground-hugging filler and needs little pinching.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in sun to part shade.

directions: Take tip cuttings and root them before transplanting into warm soil. The green leaves are boldly splashed with hot pink and rose, brightest in good light. Pinch regularly to keep the color-packed foliage full and compact.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in full sun for a tight carpet.

directions: Root the small cuttings in moist media and set them out once frost has passed. The tiny burgundy-red leaves form a fine, mat-like texture ideal for edging and knot gardens. Shear lightly to keep the carpet even and dense.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings or divisions out after frost in full sun.

directions: Propagate from cuttings or divisions and plant into warm, well-drained soil. The small leaves come in blends of green, red, orange, and yellow and take well to shearing. Trim it regularly to keep the low, formal carpet used in carpet bedding.

planting.note: Transplant after frost in full sun for richest leaf color.

directions: Root stem cuttings in a warm, humid spot and transplant into warm soil. The broad pointed leaves are a deep purple that holds strongly in full sun. Pinch the shoots for a bushier plant and richer coloring.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in full sun.

directions: Root cuttings in moist media and plant into warm, free-draining soil. The small twisted leaves blend green, red, and coppery tones and respond well to clipping. Shear it into low ribbons or patterns for traditional carpet bedding.

planting.note: Plant tubers only after soil has warmed well past the last frost.

directions: Set the knobby tubers about two inches deep with the bumpy side facing up in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Keep the ground consistently damp and feed lightly through summer to push a steady flush of leaves. Expect the bright white, green-veined foliage to fill in within two to three months of planting.

planting.note: Wait for warm nights before planting the tubers outdoors.

directions: Bury tubers two inches deep in fertile, well-drained but moist soil. This is one of the more sun-tolerant selections, though it still colors best with some afternoon shade and steady watering. The large leaves show a red central blotch with pink freckles and reach full size by midsummer.

planting.note: Plant once soil temperatures hold above about 70 F.

directions: Plant the tubers two inches deep in humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist. Provide dappled light and shelter from strong wind, which can tatter the broad leaves. The pink, green-margined foliage veined in rose develops fully by the middle of the growing season.

planting.note: Delay planting until the soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Set tubers two inches deep in rich, moisture-holding soil in a shaded bed. Keep the root zone damp and mulch to conserve moisture during hot weather. The heart-shaped white leaves laced with green veins are at their best in filtered light through the summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant tubers two inches deep in fertile, evenly moist soil; this dwarf strap-leaf type tolerates more sun than most. Keep it watered and it forms a dense, low mound of ruffled rose-pink leaves. It fills a container or edging in about two months.

planting.note: Plant into warm soil once nights stay mild.

directions: Set the small tubers about two inches deep in rich, damp soil. This compact dwarf holds chartreuse leaves speckled with maroon and works well at the front of a shaded border. Keep it consistently moist and it mounds up quickly through summer.

planting.note: Plant after the soil has warmed thoroughly.

directions: Plant tubers two inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in dappled shade. Keep well watered and feed monthly through the growing season. The silvery-white strap leaves flecked with maroon and edged in green develop over about two months.

planting.note: Set out tubers only after the danger of frost has fully passed.

directions: Plant tubers two inches deep in rich, evenly moist soil in a shaded spot. Keep the soil damp and shelter the plant from wind and hot afternoon sun. The white-centered leaves with wide green borders reach full display by midsummer.

planting.note: Plant tubers when the soil is reliably warm.

directions: Set tubers two inches deep in fertile, moisture-retentive soil. Provide filtered light and steady water to bring out the deep red leaves edged in green. The bold foliage colors up strongly as summer heat builds.

planting.note: Plant into warm soil after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant tubers two inches deep in rich, damp soil in partial shade. This compact selection holds solid red leaves rimmed with a thin green edge. Keep it evenly watered and it forms a tidy clump within two to three months.

planting.note: Plant once soil warmth is dependable.

directions: Set tubers two inches deep in fertile, moist soil in dappled light. Keep the bed damp and sheltered for the best color. Each leaf carries a pink center that fades to white then green at the margin, filling in over about two months.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost into warm soil.

directions: Plant tubers four to six inches deep in rich, wet ground and keep them soaked. The cupped leaves fold upward and collect rainwater, tipping over when full, held on dark stems. Provide full sun and steady moisture for the tallest, most upright growth.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost into warm, moist soil.

directions: Plant tubers four to six inches deep in rich, wet soil in sun to part shade. Keep constantly moist for best growth on this compact selection. Green leaves are marked with dark purple veins and burgundy undersides, held on maroon-mottled stems.

planting.note: Best grown in a pot and moved outdoors only in warm weather.

directions: Pot in a chunky, free-draining mix and keep lightly moist but never soggy. This compact hybrid grows best as a houseplant or shaded patio plant in warm, humid air. The glossy dark leaves have bold white veins and wavy silver-edged margins; give it bright indirect light for the best color.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost into rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in part shade to filtered sun. This robust hybrid holds large, dark blackish-green leaves with pale veins and silvery undersides. Keep it evenly moist and feed monthly for an imposing upright clump.

planting.note: Grow as a potted plant in warm, humid conditions.

directions: Pot in a loose, airy mix and keep lightly moist with high humidity. The thick, metallic coppery leaves have a puckered, almost reflective surface with dark veins. Give bright indirect light and steady warmth for the richest sheen.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost into rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, rich, evenly moist soil in part shade. This large species is somewhat cold-tolerant and may return from the roots in mild-winter zones. It carries broad upright green leaves and, on mature plants, fragrant spathe flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost into rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, fertile, consistently moist soil in bright filtered light. This large hybrid produces glossy, ribbed green leaves on tall upright stems. Feed generously and keep well watered for a bold tropical clump.

planting.note: Plant corms after frost into warm, rich soil.

directions: Plant corms four to six inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil; unlike taro it prefers damp ground rather than standing water. The large arrow-shaped leaves are held with the tips pointing outward on stout stems. Feed through summer and keep watered for lush, upright growth.
